9.1 Monosaccharides

Diverse Functions of Carbohydrates
Carbohydrates have diverse functions:
Storage and generation of energy (e.g., glucose, glycogen, starch)
Molecular recognition (e.g., immune system)
Cellular protection (e.g., bacterial and plant cell walls)
Cell adhesion (e.g., glycoproteins)
Biological lubrication (e.g., glycosaminoglycans)
Building and maintaining biological structure (e.g., cellulose, chitin)

Carbohydrate Terminology (1 of 3)
Monosaccharide: Simple sugars and derivatives with 3 to 9 carbon atoms
Oligosaccharide: Compound formed by linking several monosaccharides together (e.g., disaccharide, with 2)
Polysaccharide: Polymer formed from multiple saccharide units; may be homopolysaccharide or heteropolysaccharide
Glycan: Generic term for oligosaccharides and polysaccharides

Carbohydrate Terminology (2 of 3)
General formula:
When
formaldehyde
When
acetaldehyde
gives compounds with properties of sugars
There are two classes of monosaccharides:
Aldehydes are aldoses and ketones are ketoses

Carbohydrate Terminology (3 of 3)
Representative carbohydrates

Aldoses and Ketoses
Glyceraldehyde is an aldose and dihydroxyacetone is a
ketose. Both are trioses
Monosaccharides
with four carbons are called tetroses, with five pentoses, with six hexoses, and with seven heptoses.

Enantiomers (1 of 3)
Monosaccharides are chiral
for example, the second carbon of glyceraldehyde carries four different substituents
Enantiomers are optical isomers that are (nonsuperimposable) mirror images
glyceraldehyde has two enantiomers designated as D and L (or alternatively, R and S)

Enantiomers (2 of 3)
Fischer projections (see top) are the most compact way to represent stereochemistry
The wedge-dash representations of the ᴅ- and L-forms glyceraldehyde are also shown (bottom)

Enantiomers (3 of 3)
The R–S Nomenclature exemplified for Glyceraldehyde

Diastereomers (1 of 3)
Compounds with more than one asymmetric carbon may be enantiomers (mirror images) or diastereomers
diastereomers are optical isomers that are not mirror images
enantiomers have three or more carbons, while diastereomers have four or more carbons
By convention, D and L refer to configuration about the asymmetric carbon farthest from the carbonyl carbon

Diastereomers (2 of 3)
Aldotetroses
For example, ᴅ-Threose and L-Erythrose are diastereomers

Diastereomers (3 of 3)
Ketotetroses
The ketotetrose erythrulose has only two enantiomers and no diastereomers
Stereochemical Relationships of Aldoses
Stereochemical Relationships of Ketoses

Ring Structures (1 of 5)
Sugars can cyclize to form five-membered (furanose) and six-membered (pyranose) rings
Cyclization (ring formation) creates a new asymmetric center (called anomeric center) and the related
stereoisomers are designated as
Formed furanose or pyranose rings are shown in Haworth projection

Ring Structures (2 of 5)
Formation of ring structures by pentoses

Ring Structures (3 of 5)
Conformational isomers of
Actual stereochemistry resulting from the fact that the ring cannot be planar; two of several conformation isomers are shown

Ring Structures (4 of 5)
The four most common hexoses
Glucose and mannose are epimers and their isomers differing only in configuration about one carbon (C 2). Glucose and galactose are also epimers (C 4 configuration is different)

Ring Structures (5 of 5)
Pyranose ring in chair and boat conformations
Conformational isomers are compounds with the same stereochemical configuration, but differing in the three-dimensional conformation (bond rotation)
Terminology for Carbohydrate Stereochemistry (1 of 3)
Terminology for Carbohydrate Stereochemistry (2 of 3)
Terminology for Carbohydrate Stereochemistry (3 of 3)
9.2 Derivatives of the Monosaccharides

Phosphate Esters
Sugar phosphates are important intermediates in metabolism, functioning as activated compounds in syntheses

Lactones and Sugar Acids
Oxidation of monosaccharides
Monosaccharides can be oxidized at the C 1 to yield aldonic acids, which are in equilibrium with the lactone forms

Oxidation of Monosaccharides
Aldonic acid and its lactone form
Monosaccharides can also be oxidized at the C6 to yield uronic acid, such as

Alditols
Reduction of the sugar carbonyl yields an alditol
When glucose is reduced, ᴅ-glucitol (also called sorbitol) is formed

Amino Sugars (1 of 2)
are carbohydrates in which at least one hydroxyl group has been replaced with an amine group
are found in many polysaccharides and glycoproteins

Amino Sugars (2 of 2)
Derivatives of

Glycosides (1 of 2)
The elimination of water between the hydroxyl group of the anomeric carbon of a cyclic saccharide and the hydroxyl group of another compound yields an O-glycoside
This type of bond is called glycosidic bond

Glycosides (2 of 2)
Two naturally occurring glycosides

Monosaccharides and Their Abbreviations
Table 9.1 Abbreviations for some common monosaccharide residues
9.3 Oligosaccharides

Distinguishing Features of Disaccharides
There are four major features of disaccharides:
The sugar monomers involved and their stereochemistry
The carbons involved in the linkage
The order of sugars (determined by the chemical reactivity of functional groups involved in linkage)
The configuration of the anomeric carbon
Complete abbreviation for sucrose:

Writing the Structure of Disaccharides (1 of 3)
Start by putting the nonreducing end on the left with abbreviated monosaccharide name
Designate anomeric and enantiomeric forms by prefixes
Indicate the ring configuration by a suffix (p for pyranose and f for furanose; often omitted)
The carbons involved in glycosidic bond formation are numbered as in open structures of aldoses or ketoses, and
the connections are indicated by an arrow

Stability and Formation of Glycosidic Bonds (1 of 3)
Glycosidic bonds are formed between monomeric saccharides by condensation reaction, which involves the elimination of water:
However, the reaction (as written) is thermodynamically unfavored

Stability and Formation of Glycosidic Bonds (2 of 3)
The
for glycosidic bond formation is about +15 k J/mol,
therefore activation is needed
In lactose biosynthesis, the activated sugar is U D P-galactose
UDP-galactose is used as a high-energy derivative of galactose that condenses with glucose to form lactose

Stability and Formation of Glycosidic Bonds (3 of 3)
Enzymatic formation of lactose
9.4 Polysaccharides

Homopolysaccharides and Heteropolysaccharides
Polysaccharides can be distinguished into homopolysaccharides and heterosaccharides:
Homopolysaccharides are made of one type of monomer, while heterosaccharides are made of more than one type of monomer
Functionally, polysaccharides can be divided into
energy storage polysaccharides (e.g., starch and glycogen)
structural polysaccharides (e.g., cellulose)
lubricants (e.g., some glycoaminoglycans)

Energy Storage Polysaccharides
Starch (amylose and amylopectin) and glycogen

Starch (Amylose and Amylopectin) and Glycogen (1 of 3)
Starch (plants) contains both amylopectin,
glucose
polymer with
branches, and amylose,
unbranched polymer
Glycogen (animals/microbes) is like amylopectin, but higher M W with shorter and more frequent branch points
Starch (Amylose and Amylopectin) and Glycogen (2 of 3)

Starch (Amylose and Amylopectin) and Glycogen (3 of 3)
Secondary structure of amylose
The orientation of successive glucose residues favors the formation of a helix with a large interior core and stabilized by hydrogen bonds

Structural Polysaccharides (1 of 3)
Cellulose is the major structural polysaccharide in woody and fibrous plants, while chitin is found as a structural polysaccharide in most fungi and some algae, but also in many mollusks and arthropods (exoskeleton)
Cellulose is a linear homopolymer of ᴅ-glucose that is
connected by
linkages
Chitin is a homopolymer of N-acetyl-

Structural Polysaccharides (2 of 3)
Cellulose
Cellulose is a linear glucose polymer with

Structural Polysaccharides (2 of 3)
Cellulose
Cellulose is a linear glucose polymer with
linkages.

Cellulose
Organization of plant cell walls

Structural Polysaccharides (3 of 3)
Chitin
Homopolymer of N-acetyl-
Major structural component of the exoskeleton of many arthropods and mollusks
Comparable to collagen in providing a matrix for mineralization

Glycosaminoglycans  (1 of 2)
are polymers of repeating disaccharide units
serve structural and nonstructural purposes invertebrates
are important parts of connective, epithelial and neural tissues, as well as the skin
form matrices to hold together certain proteins in skin and connective tissues
act as viscosity-increasing agents or lubricants (in certain body fluids)
function as anticoagulants (e.g., heparin)

Glycosaminoglycans (2 of 2)
Representative structures

Peptidoglycan (1 of 3)
Bacterial (in particular, gram-positive) cell walls have cross-linked, multilayered polysaccharide-peptide complexes called peptidoglycan at the surface
Long chains of strictly alternating copolymers of N-acetylglucosamine (N A G) and N-acetylmuramic acid (N A M) are crosslinked through short peptides
Peptidoglycan synthesis is the site of action of the earliest antibiotics (such as penicillin)
Peptidoglycan (2 of 3)

Peptidoglycan (3 of 3)
Inhibition of the crosslinking reaction in peptidoglycan synthesis by penicillin
9.5 Glycoproteins

Linking Saccharide Chains to Proteins (1 of 2)
More than half of all eukaryotic proteins are glycoproteins, that is, they carry covalently attached oligosaccharide or polysaccharide chains
These chains can be N-linked (to the side chain amide group of an asparagine residue) or O-linked (to the side chain of the hydroxyl groups of threonine or serine residues)
Glycoproteins have a variety of different functions such as in cellular protein distribution, cell adhesion, or cell recognition

Linking Saccharide Chains to Proteins (2 of 2)
N-linked and O-linked glycoproteins

N-Linked and O-Linked Glycoproteins
Blood-group antigens are O-linked glycoproteins
Type A has GalNAc glycosyltransferase activity,while type B has Gal glycosyltransferase activity
Type AB has both, while type O has neither
The ABO is one of (at least) 14 genetically characterized blood group systems, with more than 100 different antigens, many found in tissues other than blood

Blood-Group Antigens are O-Linked Glycoproteins
The A B O blood types

The ABO Blood Types
Table 9.3 Transfusion relationships among ABO blood types
principle, this relationship is true. However, Abs are not given donations
from other types because the donor’s antibodies could react with the recipient’s antigens, unless the red blood cells are first separated from the plasma.

Erythropoetin A (EPO)
is a glycoprotein with both O- and N-linked oligosaccharides, which help stabilizing the protein core in the blood
Hormone is synthesized in kidney that stimulates the production of red blood cells
EPO is administered during cancer chemotherapy to counteract anemia
Recombinant EPO is misused by some athletes to improve performance

Influenza Virus Neuraminidase (1 of 2)
Release of influenza virus particles at the conclusion of a cycle of infection requires neuraminidase action to cleave sialic acid (or N-acetylneuraminic acid) from the remainder of N-linked oligosaccharide chains

Influenza Virus Neuraminidase (2 of 2)
Oseltamivir, marketed as Tamiflu, is an analog of the sialic acid substrate
It inhibits neuraminidase activity and thus blocks the release of newly formed virus particles from infected cells
9.6 Tools of Biochemistry

Glycomics
Because of the large variety of monomers and branching possibilities, structural analysis of polysaccharides is difficult
In addition to the development of improved analytical techniques, the field of glycomics has been facilitated by the characterization of enzymes that digest specific links in polysaccharides

Chapter 9 Summary (1 of 2)
Carbohydrates (or saccharides) are compounds with the
general stoichiometric formula
They have one or more chiral centers and exist as enantiomers or diastereomers
Monosaccharides may be aldoses or ketoses
When containing five or more carbons, they usually form rings with five (furanose) or six (pyranose) atoms, which
exist as
anomers and exhibit multiple
conformations
Derivatives of monosaccharides include phosphate esters, sugar acids, amino sugars, and glycosides

Chapter 9 Summary (2 of 2)
Monosaccharides can be linked by glycosidic bonds to form di-, oligo-, or polysaccharides
The condensation reaction linking monosaccharides is thermodynamically unfavored under physiological conditions and requires “activated” (i.e., phosphorylated) substrates
Polysaccharides are used for various purposes, including energy storage (starch and glycogen) and cellular structures (cellulose and chitin)
Oligo- and polysaccharides can also be linked to proteins to form glycoproteins
